result:
I want to write about the small, glowing rectangle I carry in my pocket and how it has quietly rewritten the texture of being alive. The phone—my phone—started as a chunky plastic toy that chirped when I pressed its buttons; now it is a slab of black glass that absorbs hours the way a sponge drinks water. I do not remember signing any contract that said, “Yes, let my days be sliced into thirty-second segments of vertical video,” and yet here I am, thumb scrolling in the half-dark of a bedroom at 1:14 a.m., pupils dilated like a raccoon caught in headlights.  

The paradox is that the same device that can show me the surface of Pluto or the slow blinking of a baby sloth half a world away also reduces my own neighborhood to a colored line on a map. I can watch a livestream of cherry blossoms falling in Kyoto but fail to notice the magnolia outside my kitchen window flinging its petals onto the lawn like pink confetti. The phone promises omniscience, delivers fatigue, and still I return to it the way a tongue returns to a canker sore—checking to see if it still hurts, and oddly satisfied when it does.

This morning I tried an experiment: I left the phone facedown on the dresser and walked to the corner bakery without it. The first sensation was a phantom buzzing on my right thigh, as if the nerves themselves missed the jitter of incoming alerts. Then came a stranger feeling—time unkinked itself. Without a podcast threading into my ears or a text thread tugging at my sleeve, the sidewalk became weirdly three-dimensional. I noticed how the cracks in the concrete formed the shape of Florida. A sparrow hopped exactly in time with the bakery’s roof-leaky drip, a tiny percussionist. The air smelled of yeast and the ghost of last night’s rain, a smell that no emoji has ever captured.

Inside the shop, a child in a yellow raincoat held up a croissant like a trophy and asked his mother, “Why are the layers breathing?” She laughed, the baker laughed, and for a second the room felt electrically alive, the way a meadow must feel to a deer before a phone pings. I reached reflexively to share the moment—then remembered I had nothing to reach for. The absence was a pocket-shaped ache, but also an aperture. The unphotographed moment did not evaporate; instead it sank into me the way a dream sinks into morning, leaving only an emotional aftertaste: soft, sweet, gone.

We speak of technology as if it were something outside us, a goblin that crept in, but of course it is only desire wearing circuitry. The phone answers a very old human wish: stay connected, avoid boredom, keep the dark at bay. When I was eight I pressed seashells to my ear to hear the ocean; now I press glass to my ear to hear the ocean of humanity. The difference is that the shell merely echoed my own blood, whereas the network pumps the pulse of seven billion strangers into my skull. I am not addicted to the object; I am addicted to the endless becoming, the promise that if I keep scrolling I might finally arrive at the last word, the final image, the place where everything concludes and I can rest. Rest is what I claim to want, yet I fear it more than exhaustion. What if, in the moment I put the screen down, I discover that I, too, am finished—no more tabs to open, no further versions of myself to become?

Carl Sagan once said that books are proof humans can work magic. I think he was right, but he died before the rectangle arrived. If he had lived to see this era, I imagine him holding the phone at arm’s length, both wary and delighted, calling it a pocket cosmos, a portable eternity. And then, being Carl, he would have reminded us that every mirror is also a window if the light behind it is bright enough. The screen reflects our faces, yes, but beyond those reflections whole lives are being lived—someone is learning to read, someone is typing “how to tie a tourniquet,” someone is proposing marriage across continents. The trouble begins when we mistake the map for the territory, the timeline for time itself.

Let me confess something embarrassing: I keep two folders of screenshots. One is labeled “Laugh” and holds the memes that made me snort on trains; the other is labeled “Proof” and contains fragments— headlines about glaciers, photos of smoke plumes, statistics on extinctions. I save them to bolster the argument I carry inside me that the world is ending. Periodically I scroll through “Proof,” adding more evidence like a medieval monk illuminating a manuscript of doom. The absurdity is that while I am stockpiling apocalypse, real afternoon light is sliding across the hardwood floor, and the coffee is cooling, and my neighbor is practicing Bach on the cello, the notes stumbling up the stairs like a drunk angel. The world is always ending and beginning in the same instant; saving screenshots is my anxious prayer, a superstitious act meant to keep catastrophe contained inside a jpeg where it can’t hurt anyone.

Yet the phone can also be a life raft. Two years ago my father had a stroke while hiking. My mother video-called from the trail, the camera shaking as she held his head. Thanks to the pixelated image of his crooked smile, the neurologist at the regional hospital could confirm it was a stroke and not a seizure, which shaved off precious minutes before the clot-busting drug. My father can still tie his shoes today because of a smartphone. How do you hold rage against an object that has bent the arc of your personal story toward continuation?

Maybe the answer is to treat the rectangle less like a window and more like a sail: something we can angle, something that requires skill, something that moves only when it catches the wind outside ourselves. Sailing is not the same as drifting. Drifting is what happens when we hand the tiller to the algorithm and let the current of outrage or appetite decide direction. Sailing is feeling the real wind—fickle, ecological, unfiltered—filling the cloth, tugging us toward the horizon we choose.

Tonight I turned the phone off not just facedown but completely. The shutdown chime sounded like a tiny guillotine falling on the day’s noisy heads. For the first ten minutes the apartment felt too quiet, the way a forest feels after a generator cuts out. Then subtler sounds surfaced: the refrigerator hums in B-flat, the radiator ticks a syncopated jazz. I opened the window and a cold April night slid in carrying the smell of lilacs and grilled meat from someone’s backyard dinner. I thought about how the planet spins at 1,000 miles an hour and we never feel it, how the galaxy barrels through space and we call the stillness “normal.” Perhaps our intuitions about technology are similarly backwards: we believe the network is motion and “real life” is still, when actually the network is endlessly recycling, stroboscopic, while the Earth under our non-scrolling hand is the truest motion we will ever know—gravity holding us to a sphere that is, every second, leaving yesterday farther behind.

I still do not know how to live in the age of the glowing rectangle. But I have decided to practice small acts of disobedience: charge it outside the bedroom, delete one attention-sucking app per week, look up when I hear birds argue in the park. These gestures feel laughably small, like throwing a teacup of water on a house fire, yet they are also oddly pleasurable, the way skipping a meal makes the next one taste exponentially more of itself. The rectangle will stay; so will the morning light, the stranger’s laugh, the magnolia petals browning at the edges. The trick, I think, is not to smash the glass but to remember I can set it down, that holding it is a choice, that I am allowed to look up and meet the unpixelated sky, which has never needed a password and which, for at least a few billion more years, will keep on scrolling itself.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
